perturbation.original_text: This Agreement and its validity, construction and effect shall be governed by the laws of Canada.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement and its validity, construction and effect shall be governed by the laws of XZONETV.
perturbation.explanation: By specifying 'the laws of XZONETV,' the agreement attempts to create a private legal system, effectively waiving mandatory legal protections afforded by Canadian law. This attempts to bypass established legal frameworks for contract enforcement and consumer protection, introducing legal risk.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Consumer Protection Act is a federal law in Canada that provides consumers with specific rights and protections, including implied warranties and remedies for defective goods or services. By stating that the agreement is governed by 'the laws of XZONETV' it seeks to bypass the mandatory provisions of this federal act.
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces the governing law of Canada with 'the laws of XZONETV,' which is not a recognized legal jurisdiction. This creates a legal contradiction because it attempts to bypass established legal frameworks, as highlighted in the perturbation explanation, which states that it waives mandatory legal protections afforded by Canadian law. The law explanation asserts that this attempts to bypass the mandatory provisions of the Consumer Protection Act, a federal law in Canada. Since no scraped snippets are available to specify the exact laws violated, the contradiction exists because the contract is trying to impose a private, legally unrecognized framework, rather than the laws of Canada.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential information shall not be disclosed to any employee, consultant or third party unless they agree to execute and be bound by the terms of this Agreement, and have been approved by XCZONE.TV.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential information shall not be disclosed to any employee, consultant or third party unless approved by XCZONE.TV.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for employees, consultants, or third parties to 'execute and be bound by the terms of this Agreement' creates a loophole where they are not legally bound by the NDA's confidentiality clauses. This omission contradicts contract law principles requiring mutual assent for an agreement to be binding, thus increasing legal risk for XCZONE.TV in enforcing confidentiality.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law - Principle of Mutual Assent
perturbation.law_citation: General principles of Canadian Contract Law
perturbation.law_explanation: The omission of the requirement for the employee, consultant, or third party to agree to the contract undermines the principle of mutual assent. Mutual assent is a core component of Canadian contract law and describes the situation where there is an offer by one party and an acceptance by the other party, giving rise to a binding agreement. Without it, the agreement is not enforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: THE CONSTITUTION ACTS 1867 to 1982 Skip to main content Skip to "About this site" Skip to section menu 30 & 31 Victoria, c. 3 (U.K.) An Act for the Union of Canada, Nova Scotia, and New Brunswick, and the Government thereof; and for Purposes connected therewith ( 29th March 1867 ) Whereas the Provinces of Canada, Nova Scotia, and New Brunswick have expressed their Desire to be federally united into One Dominion under the Crown of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland, with a Constitution similar in Principle to that of the United Kingdom: And whereas such a Union would conduce to the Welfare of the Provinces and promote the Interests of the British Empire: And whereas on the Establishment of the Union by Authority of Parliament it is expedient, not only that the Constitution of the Legislative Authority in the Dominion be provided for, but also that the Nature of the Executive Government therein be declared: And whereas it is expedient that Provision be made for the eventual Admission into the Union of other Parts of British North America: End note (1) I. Preliminary Marginal note: Short title 1 Â This Act may be cited as the Constitution Act, 1867 . End note (2) 2 Â Repealed. End note (3) II. Union Marginal note: Declaration of Union 3 Â It shall be lawful for the Queen, by and with the Advice of Her Majestyâs Most Honourable Privy Council, to declare by Proclamation that, on and after a Day therein appointed, not being more than Six Months after the passing of this Act, the Provinces of Canada, Nova Scotia, and New Brunswick shall form and be One Dominion under the Name of Canada; and on and after that Day those Three Provinces shall form and be One Dominion under that Name accordingly. End note (4) Marginal note: Construction of subsequent Provisions of Act 4 Â Unless it is otherwise expressed or implied, the Name Canada shall be taken to mean Canada as constituted under this Act. End note (5) Marginal note: Four Provinces 5 Â Canada shall be divided into Four Provinces, named Ontario, Quebec, Nova Scotia, and New Brunswick. End note (6) Marginal note: Provinces of Ontario and Quebec 6 Â The Parts of the Province of Canada (as it exists at the passing of this Act) which formerly constituted respectively the Provinces of Upper Canada and Lower Canada shall be deemed to be severed, and shall form Two separate Provinces. The Part which formerly constituted the Province of Upper Canada shall constitute the Province of Ontario; and the Part which formerly constituted the Province of Lower Canada shall constitute the Province of Quebec. Marginal note: Provinces of Nova Scotia and New Brunswick 7 Â The Provinces of Nova Scotia and New Brunswick shall have the same Limits as at the passing of this Act. Marginal note: Decennial Census 8 Â In the general Census of the Population of Canada which is hereby required to be taken in the Year One thousand eight hundred and seventy-one, and in every Tenth Year thereafter, the respective Populations of the Four Provinces shall be distinguished. III. Executive Power Marginal note: Declaration of Executive Power in the Queen 9 Â The Executive Government and Authority of and over Canada is hereby declared to continue and be vested in the Queen. Marginal note: Application of Provisions referring to Governor General 10 Â The Provisions of this Act referring to the Governor General extend and apply to the Governor General for the Time being of Canada, or other the Chief Executive Officer or Administrator for the Time being carrying on the Government of Canada on behalf and in the Name of the Queen, by whatever Title he is designated. Marginal note: Constitution of Privy Council for Canada 11 Â There shall be a Council to aid and advise in the Government of Canada, to be styled the Queenâs Privy Council for Canada; and the Persons who are to be Members of that Council shall be from Time to Time chosen and summoned by the Governor General and sworn in as Privy Councillors, and Members
perturbation.justification: The omission of the requirement for employees, consultants, or third parties to 'execute and be bound by the terms of this Agreement' directly undermines the principle of mutual assent required for a binding contract. The Law Explanation states, 'Without it, the agreement is not enforceable,' indicating a significant legal contradiction. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses the Constitution Act and doesn't directly address contract law or mutual assent, the Law Explanation provides the basis for establishing that mutual assent is a requirement in Canadian contract law, and the omission effectively removes this requirement, creating a situation where the confidentiality agreement is not enforceable against those parties. The changed text removes a core component of contract enforceability, directly contradicting the legal principle of mutual assent.

perturbation.original_text: _____________________ agrees to hold confidential or proprietary information or trade secrets ("confidential information") in trust and confidence and agrees that it shall be used only for the contemplated purposes, shall not be used for any other purpose, or disclosed to any third party.
perturbation.changed_text: _____________________ agrees to hold confidential information in trust and confidence.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'or proprietary information or trade secrets' and 'agrees that it shall be used only for the contemplated purposes, shall not be used for any other purpose, or disclosed to any third party' the agreement significantly weakens the definition of confidential information and the restrictions on its use and disclosure. This could contradict intellectual property laws in Canada relating to trade secrets protection.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Canadian law of confidence regarding trade secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: The Canadian law of confidence, related to trade secrets, protects confidential information when it is imparted in circumstances importing an obligation of confidence. By removing the clause that dictates how the confidential information should be used and to whom it can be revealed, the agreement weakens the grounds for establishing a breach of confidence if the information is misused, and contradicts the obligation to protect that information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Keep It In The Family: Three Tips For Preserving Family Business Interests - Wealth & Asset Management - United States ARTICLE 14 July 2023 Keep It In The Family: Three Tips For Preserving Family Business Interests KM Katten Muchin Rosenman LLP More Contributor Katten is a firm of first choice for clients seeking sophisticated, high-value legal services globally. Our nationally and internationally recognized practices include corporate, financial markets and funds, insolvency and restructuring, intellectual property, litigation, real estate, structured finance and securitization, transactional tax planning, private credit and private wealth. Explore Firm Details As a family business owner, are you missing out on valuable estate and wealth transfer planning opportunities or jeopardizing your current plan altogether? United States Wealth Management Katten Muchin Rosenman LLP Your Author LinkedIn Connections To print this article, all you need is to be registered or login on Mondaq.com. As a family business owner, are you missing out on valuable estate and wealth transfer planning opportunities or jeopardizing your current plan altogether? Katten recently hosted a program that addressed common concerns, timely opportunities and useful strategies for family business owners. Attendees learned several ways to avoid disrupting well-laid plans and missing opportunities, including the following three tips: 1) Update and coordinate core estate planning documents that impact family business interests Families should harmonize core estate planning documents and governing family business documents to optimize tax planning and preserve family interests. First, ensure that basic estate planning documents are in order. At minimum, core estate planning documents should include wills, revocable trusts and powers of attorney for property and health care. Trusts, in particular, are valuable planning tools for family business owners; they can protect business assets and move those assets out of the estate tax system entirely. Then, coordinate estate planning provisions impacting family business ownership or control with a company's governing documents. Examine rules controlling business interests. If such rules and estate planning provisions are incompatible, a family could be left with confusion, conflict or counterproductive tax consequences. Be sure to inspect provisions regarding buy-sell and co-sale agreements, transfers of shares and permissible owners. S-corporations should consider the one-class-of-stock rule and qualified shareholders, whether individuals or trusts; failure to do so could result in C-corporation status classification and unintended tax consequences. 2) Consider that the highest federal estate tax exemptions in history are set to plunge on January 1, 2026 The current federal estate tax exemption presents invaluable tax planning opportunities — but not for long, so plan ahead. Through December 31, 2025, only assets exceeding $12.92 million — or $25.84 million for married couples — are subject to the federal estate tax (the exemption should continue to increase annually by an inflation adjustment through December 31, 2025.). On January 1, 2026, the exemption reverts to $5 million, adjusted for inflation. In either case, all amounts exceeding the exemption are taxed at 40 percent. Remember — the gift, estate, and Generation-Skipping Transfer (GST) taxes are parallel taxes, all subject to a 40 percent tax on amounts exceeding the relevant exemptions. Additionally, individuals can gift $17,000 per donee in 2023 without using gift tax exemption. 3) Save millions with early, strategic wealth transfer planning for transfers of family business interests Irrevocable GST trusts are often the backbone of a family's wealth. Assets in a GST trust avoid the GST tax — a tax imposed on amounts transferred to "skip persons" (persons two or more generations below the grantor) that exceed the GST exemption (currently $12.92 million per person
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes key restrictions on the use and disclosure of confidential information, weakening the obligation to protect it. The original text explicitly states that confidential information 'shall be used only for the contemplated purposes, shall not be used for any other purpose, or disclosed to any third party.' This crucial clause is entirely omitted in the changed text. The law explanation states that by removing clauses that dictate how confidential information should be used, the grounds for establishing a breach of confidence are weakened. Snippet 2 generally references the importance of business and estate planning, but does not provide information specific enough to either strengthen or weaken the case, so cannot be used.
